data.title: Where Are We Currently Finding Value?
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Fiduciary Management Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook.

Despite the challenging backdrop, we continue to find attractive investment opportunities. The following are a few stock examples where we are currently finding value:

Hayward Holdings Inc. (HAYW) – Small Cap

Hayward Holdings is a leading global pool equipment manufacturer, primarily serving the residential pool market. North America accounts for 85% of sales and over 90% of profits. Product categories include Pumps, Automation and Sanitization, Heaters, Filters, Lighting and Water Features, and Cleaners. The company estimates that 80% of total sales come from their existing installed base of pools (50% repair and replacement), making the business relatively resilient to economic cycles. Hayward also exhibits strong pricing power as equipment purchase decisions are typically made by pool service professionals, who are less sensitive to price than the end customer. Pool service professionals also tend to be risk averse and primarily purchase equipment from the leading players in the industry. These dynamics lead to high margins and ROIC. The pool industry went through a period of supercharged growth in the early parts of Covid, which gave way to a period of extremely weak end-market demand, exacerbated by destocking in the distribution channel. The industry has since stabilized but is still experiencing lackluster demand for newly built and remodeled pools. We believe that over time, the discretionary sides of the business should recover. In the meantime, Hayward should be able to continue raising prices and growing volumes within the installed base of pools. They have a solid balance sheet, strong management team, and reasonable valuation multiple, particularly given their depressed earnings.

Accenture Plc Ci A (ACN) – Large Cap/All Cap/Global

Accenture is the world's leading IT consultant, with advantages stemming from their depth and breadth across products, geographies, and industries. Their revenue is split roughly in half between IT consulting and managed services. Over the last four years, Accenture's valuation has roughly halved. They've faced headwinds in IT spending and suffered from the perception that they are an AI loser. We believe that AI will cause deflationary pressure in parts of their business, but that it will be more than offset by the work required for enterprises to adopt AI. This is recently evidenced by partnerships with OpenAI and Anthropic. The AI supplier landscape is increasingly fragmented, and corporate customers need significant help adopting these technologies at scale. We believe this will drive AI suppliers and customers into Accenture's arms. The current cyclical pressures are being attributed to structural issues, which we believe is incorrect, creating an attractive long-term set-up.

Smiths Group PLC (Smin Ln) (SMGZY) – International

Smiths is making tangible progress in its transition toward a more focused, higher-performing portfolio of industrial technology businesses. The Medical segment was divested in January 2022, with the exits of Interconnect and Detection expected to be complete in 2026. In parallel, management has simplified the operating structure and driven meaningful cost and capital efficiency improvements. The pro forma company (John Crane and Flex-Tek) is positioned for structurally higher growth, margins, and returns than the legacy conglomerate, underpinned by more concentrated exposure to flow-management and thermal-solutions markets, a high proportion of recurring revenue, and an active operational-excellence agenda. Their improved growth and profitability profile is complemented by a pristine balance sheet and substantial shareholder returns. While the shares have performed well recently, Smiths continues to trade at a modest valuation relative to its fundamental outlook and at a discount to its estimated break-up value.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AI: Short Circuit?
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Fiduciary Management Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook.

AI: Short Circuit?

The impact AI has had on global stock markets and economic growth is simply staggering. JPMorgan (JPM) has identified 42 AI-related stocks in the S&P 500 (SP500), which today represent 45% of the index’s market cap. They estimate that these stocks have accounted for 78% of S&P 500 returns, 66% of earnings growth, and 71% of capital spending growth since ChatGPT launched in November 2022. Let that sink in...the narrow concentration over the last three years is astounding. Keeping up with the S&P 500 without leaning heavily into AI has been a virtual impossibility, per the table below. Excluding these 42 AI-related companies, the S&P 500 would have underperformed Europe, Japan, and China over the period.

Mega cap technology companies dominate the MSCI World Index (MSCI) as well, accounting for 9 of the top 10 holdings (all are U.S. companies). The High-Tech Strategist points out that “The U.S. accounts for just 4% of the world’s population and 24% of world GDP, yet these [top] 10 stocks account for 28.3% of the total MSCI World Index weighting. The total weighting of all U.S. stocks comes to 72.7% of the index. NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for 6% of the index weight, more than all Japan’s stocks (5.5%). In country weights, Japan is second to the U.S.” This can be attributed to unwavering enthusiasm for just about anything that touches AI.

Returns, earnings and capex / R&D of AI-related stocks in the S&P 500 since ChatGPT launch in Q4 2022

Direct AI28 stocks AI Utilities8 stocks AI Cap Equip6 stocks Total AI42 stocks S&P 500ex-AI MSCIEurope MSCIJapan MSCIChina
Since November 2022
Price Return 195% 66% 174% 190% 26% 33% 59% 50%
Earnings growth 159% 64% 155% 153% 19% 4% 52% 15%
Capex / R&D growth 72% 13% 20% 68% 19% 28% 37% 27%

Share of changes since November 2022

Price Return 76% 0.8% 1.3% 78% 22%
Earnings growth 63% 1.6% 1.5% 66% 34%
Capex / R&D growth 70% 1.0% 0.2% 71% 29%
Source: Bloomberg, JPMAM, December 22, 2025

As it relates to the impact on the U.S. economy, JPM estimates tech sector capital spending contributed 40%-45% of U.S. GDP growth through the first 9 months of the year, up from less than 5% during the same period in 2023. As JPM depicts to the right, tech capex in 2025 dwarfs every major historical U.S. infrastructure project as a percentage of GDP. Well-known economist David Rosenberg pegs the AI impact on the economy as even greater, writing that “100% of the [1H25] GDP growth has come from the 8% chunk of the economy linked to the AI boom ... the other 92% is completely flat.” This is consistent with the weakness we are seeing elsewhere in the economy (i.e., employment, housing, manufacturing, construction, transportation). There are clearly cracks beneath the surface, with underlying economic growth much less robust than it appears.

Asset-Heavy Firms Underperform

As we wrote in our last letter, we see tremendous long-term potential for AI, but question whether the enormous amount of capital being spent will generate attractive returns and over what time frame. The numbers thrown around continue to mystify. OpenAI, for example, is looking to spend $1.4 trillion on AI infrastructure by 2033. The company, however, is only generating ~$20 billion in revenue, is unprofitable, burning cash, and completely dependent upon external financing. The math does not add up. OpenAI’s well-documented “circular” funding with its business partners (NVIDIA, Microsoft (MSFT), among others) is additional cause for concern.

Moreover, the top five hyperscalers are expected to spend over $500 billion on capex this year alone. The capital intensity of these businesses has sky-rocketed, with capex-to-revenue expected to reach 29% in aggregate in 2026. By way of comparison, well-known industrial company Caterpillar (CAT) has a historical capex-to-revenue of ~5%. One of the most capital-intensive industries in the world is railroads, where Union Pacific (UNP) has a comparable metric of around 14%. As illustrated below, capital intensity has not historically been well regarded by the stock market, with asset-light businesses increasingly outperforming over the last 30 years. Eventually, revenue will also need to follow. Bain Capital estimates AI companies will have to generate a whopping $2 trillion in combined revenue by 2030 to fund the computing power needed to meet expected demand, but are likely to fall short by around $800 billion. Something will have to give.

Tech capital spending in 2025 vs spending on major US infrastructure projects

Peak annual project percent of GDP

As we learned in the 2000 tech bubble, a ground-breaking technology (internet) can prove to be everything that was expected and more, but that may not be enough to mitigate the downside risks. Greenlight Capital’s David Einhorn opines, “Reflecting upon the internet bubble with the benefit of hindsight, whoever was the most bullish about the internet in the year 2000 was still not bullish enough. At the turn of the century, we had no smart phones, no tablets, no Wi-Fi, no Google, no Facebook, no TikTok, no cloud, and no video streaming. The internet has become much more important than even the biggest optimists believed.”

Importantly, that did not stop the Nasdaq (NASDAQ) index from falling 77% from its peak and 200 companies from going bankrupt.

If the AI stars do not carefully align, we could be in for a rough ride in the years ahead, with downside protection of paramount importance. In the meantime, we follow our DNA: buy strong companies that make money, avoid stressed balance sheets, stay disciplined on valuation, and keep it simple. While the market is less interested in our types of stories today, we view that as a historical anomaly. We will stay the course with confidence that better days lie ahead.

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: J.B. Hunt 2026 Outlook - Improving Pricing, Volume Risks, Increased Expectations
data.description:
The last few years have been rough for transports in general, with excess capacity and often-weak demand hitting pricing while inflationary pressures hit margins. J.B. Hunt (JBHT) has held up better than many in some respects, with its strengths in areas like intermodal and dedicated trucking allowing the company to preserve EBTIDA margins in the 13%’s during this downturn while also generating free cash flow.

It’s been a while since I’ve written about J.B. Hunt for Seeking Alpha, and the shares have certainly shown some volatility over that time. Net-net, the low-20%’s gain in the share price is a bit below that of the broader transport space and well below the industrial sector, so I don’t feel too bad about a neutral stance then given my views on the longer-term return potential.

The call is a little more challenging now. I feel better about the outlook for JBHT going into 2026, but the share price move from the $130’s to over $200 since September 2025 captures a lot of that improved outlook. I can see some upside from here, but not enough to really get me all that excited about the shares, particularly with ongoing risks to demand in the wider economy.

The Guide Will Be Important

With stocks like JBHT, it often feels like the quarterly report only matters when it’s bad. If results are good but the guidance is soft, the stock will sell off. If the guidance is good but the results are soft, the stock will often sell off. It’s only when both are good that the Street really seems to get all that excited about the quarter that was.

In any case, I’m a little cautious regarding the upcoming results. It looks like this year’s peak season was pretty solid, so that should be good for the trucking businesses, especially with the truckload business having meaningful spot exposure. On the other hand, I’m not quite as bullish on intermodal given ongoing pricing pressures and weaker rail traffic, with intermodal down 3.4% in December, down 6.5% in November, and down 3% in October.

As far as margins go, I am concerned that the year-end surge in spot rates could create pressure in the brokerage business and I think Final Mile could likewise see pressure from softer volumes. On the positive side, management made rapid initial progress in the prior quarter with its $100M/yr cost reduction program, so there could be some incremental upside here to offset some of the business pressures.

I’m definitely curious as to what the guide for the first quarter will look like, and this will set an early tone for a range of peers like Hub Group (HUBG), Knight (KNX), RXO (RXO), Schneider (SNDR), and others. Fairly steep sequential declines from Q4 to Q1 are normal with this business, and with the Street currently looking for around a 5% step down, there could be some risk here if management sees ongoing softness in intermodal volumes.

Improving Rates, Mixed Demand Drivers

There have been more than a couple false dawns in the trucking and intermodal sectors over the past couple of years has capacity remained stubbornly high despite weak prices and rising costs. Looking out across 2026, pricing does seem to finally be firming up, but the overall outlook for demand is still mixed.

I’m incrementally more bullish on the trucking parts of JBHT’s business right now. With the current administration more aggressively enforcing regulations, drivers and trucks are leaving the market, leading to recent spot rates that are the highest in about three years. This supply correction has been long in coming, but there is a risk that idle capacity at trucking companies could be activated relatively quickly (provided they can find the drivers).

As far as trucking demand goes, things are more mixed. Housing remains weak and neither consumer nor business confidence are very strong right now. Destocking across many industrial end-markets should be positive for short-cycle demand and provide some support for trucking volumes, but cost inflation remains a risk.

I’m more concerned about intermodal, though I do think there will be some improvement as the year goes on. A still-weak manufacturing environment (an ISM PMI below 50) is a concern, but again I think low inventory levels will support some improvement in activity. Weaker transcon volumes on tariffs remain a threat, and comps will be more challenging as companies bought ahead of tariffs, and I do see a risk of overcapacity in the intermodal space without a stronger recovery in manufacturing activity to offset likely weaker import-related volumes.

Self-Help Can Be A Positive Driver

I might argue that JBHT doesn’t get enough credit for its long-term history of adapting to market changes, and I think that is still a relevant issue today.

Years ago the narrative on the stock was that management was making a mistake by hanging on to its dedicated and truckload trucking businesses and that intermodal and asset-light logistics is where the future was at. Now the Dedicated segment is more profitable than Intermodal and there are broader sector-wide concerns about excess capacity in intermodal and brokerage and trucking businesses have come into relatively greater favor.

Likewise, there are bearish talking points out there about JBHT’s vulnerability to the proposed Union Pacific (UNP) Norfolk Southern (NSC) merger. While Norfolk has been an important part of JBHT’s intermodal business, I don’t think a combined UP-NS will necessarily find it so easy to just switch away from JBHT (the idea being that because JBHT doesn’t do much business with UP, they’ll look to replace JBHT on those former NS volumes, perhaps with rivals like Hub Group). Operational disruptions are no trivial thing for rail operators and JBHT’s significant presence in the market would make it difficult at best to simply stop doing business with.

In terms of self-help, I think the bears may be underestimating JBHT’s ability to offset disruptions here, whether by doing more business with CSX (CSX) or other carriers or simply reallocating assets across its footprint.

I also see more self-help potential on the IT side of things. Relative to many of its rivals, JBHT’s scale gives it the ability to invest meaningful sums into IT on a relatively consistent basis. Although I think AI is more of buzzword in logistics today than a true driver, management has continued to invest in its systems and capabilities and I do think these investments will help drive more costs out of the system and better operating performance metrics.

The Outlook

I do expect 2026 to be better than 2025, but I’m starting off the year a little below the average sell-side revenue target (about 2% below) given my ongoing concerns about persistent soft volumes. I am fairly bullish on pricing though, and management has started to finally see some pricing improvements in intermodal contracts, and I think business should accelerate through the year.

Looking beyond 2026 I’m more bullish on growth, and I’m expecting around 5% annualized revenue growth and a long-term annualized growth rate between 4% and 5%.

On the margin side, I expect a meaningful improvement in 2026 through better asset utilization, improving volumes, and the realization of those cost reduction efforts. I’m expecting EBITDA margin to improve to around 14%, with upside to around 14.5% if business develops better than I expect. Going out a few more years I think 15% margins are possible. Those margin improvements should drive free cash flow margins back into the 6% range, supporting around 7% annualized FCF growth.

Valuation is challenging, but that’s not exactly a new development with J.B. Hunt, as the shares have long traded at robust multiples (including low-to-mid-teen EBITDA multiples and P/E multiples in the low-20’s). A 13x multiple on forward EBITDA gives a little upside from here (to around $218), and I don’t rule out the possibility of beat-and-raise quarters if the economy strengthens more than I expect in 2026.

I do also want to note that JBHT management has been good about returning capital to shareholders. Unlike some companies that advertise buybacks but don’t really deliver, or simply use buybacks to offset dilution from executive stock awards, JBHT has reduced its share count by around 40% over the past two decades, and the company announced another $1B buyback authorization back in October that should reduce the share count by another 4%-6%.

The Bottom Line

J.B. Hunt is another good example of a situation where I like the company but don’t often love the stock given an often-elevated valuation. With a 10-year return of about 12.5% versus a 15.5% return for the S&P 500, a 14.5% return for the broader industrial sector, and an 11% return for the transport sector, I don’t feel like I’ve really missed out by not paying up for the shares.

Should JBHT sell off again, whether on disappointing results/guidance for Q4/Q1 or a broader sell-off in the sector, it’s a name I’d revisit. But given a still-challenging end-market outlook and a pretty robust rebound in the valuation and Street expectations, I’m not inclined to chase it now.

data.title: Fiduciary Management Q4 2025 Investment Strategy Outlook
data.description:
The intoxication with Artificial Intelligence (AI) related companies continued in 2025, in conjunction with a low-quality “junk” rally that added fuel to the fire. Investors who were willing to extend out along the risk curve were rewarded. The Russell 2000 (RUT), S&P 500 (SP500), Msci Eafe (Local/USD), and MSCI World (MSCI WORLD) gained 12.81%, 17.88%, 20.60%/31.22%, and 21.09%, respectively. Many Wall Street strategists are now penciling in greater than 10% returns for the S&P 500 again in 2026, which would mark four consecutive years of double-digit returns for only the third time in history. It last occurred in the lead-up to the dot-com crash in the late 1990s, with an earlier iteration in the post-war period (1949-52). In the current cycle, record U.S. valuations have failed to put a damper on speculative behavior. Quite the opposite, in fact.

Each of FMI’s portfolios have generated solid double-digit annual returns over the past 3 years, but as expected, have not kept pace with the broader indices given the backdrop. While FMI’s discipline on business quality, balance sheet strength, and valuation have served our clients well over our 45+ year history, these investment attributes have been shunned in the current climate. We underwrite calculated risks and remain true to our process. As the stock market exhibits increasing signs of a potential bubble, we continue to proceed with restraint and prudence.

Junkyard Rally

Equity market index providers such as S&P, Russell, and MSCI each define “quality” businesses a bit differently. A variety of factors are used for classification, including return on equity (ROE), profitability, balance sheet leverage, earnings variability, and cash flow (i.e., accruals ratio). These are generally consistent with how FMI defines quality: businesses with sustainable competitive advantages and barriers to entry, return on invested capital (ROIC) above cost of capital, strong earnings quality (cash flow generation) and predictability, and a robust balance sheet. As illustrated in the chart on the top, high-quality has underperformed low-quality sharply in the U.S. in 2025 (per the downward sloping blue line), despite outperforming over the long run.

Low-quality’s recent outperformance has been especially acute in the small cap universe, where companies that lose money, have a low ROE, do not have sales, or are high beta have dominated since the market bottomed on April 8, 2025. The Jefferies graphic on the bottom illustrates how each subset has performed versus the overall benchmark (Russell 2000, or RUT), which is eye opening. As depicted, small cap active managers have struggled to keep pace during this junk rally.

Overseas, quality has been a meaningful laggard as well, with the Msci Eafe (EAFE) Quality Index underperforming the Msci Eafe by ~10% in both local FX and USD. According to Goldman Sachs, 2025’s top performing themes in Europe were Defense (+89.9%), Rate Sensitive Financials (+72.1%), and EU Miners (+55.5%). FMI considers most of the constituents in these buckets to be at the low-end of the quality spectrum and thus has limited exposure.

Leuthold 3000 Universe is composed of the largest 3,000 securities traded on the U.S. exchanges. November 2025.

Big rally by lower-quality groups; really hurting active managers

For those with a value orientation, as we deploy at FMI, a tilt toward quality has worked quite well over the long-term. The Leuthold Group tracks “Quality Value” (cheap stocks that also rank high on quality metrics) versus other gradients of value: “Value” (cheap stocks) and “Junky Value” (cheap stocks that rank low on quality metrics). Despite the recent headwinds, Quality Value’s long-term relative outperformance is unmistakable, as illustrated below.

So why does Quality Value work?

  • Good businesses with sound balance sheets are better equipped to navigate difficult times.
  • Earning a ROIC above cost of capital creates economic value; reinvesting at high incremental returns builds a powerful compounding effect.
  • Investing in businesses with organic growth helps to avoid value traps (i.e. secular decline).
  • Buying out-of-favor businesses at a discount to intrinsic value creates a margin of safety.
  • Strong balance sheets allow for self-financing, avoiding expensive debt and equity issuance.
  • Weak balance sheets can lead to insolvency or bankruptcy in distressed situations.

Given all of the above factors, Quality Value tends to offer superior downside protection during market downturns. Ultimately, buying advantaged businesses at discount valuations is a winning formula. Rallies like we are witnessing today are typically short-lived.

AI: Short Circuit?

The impact AI has had on global stock markets and economic growth is simply staggering. JPMorgan (JPM) has identified 42 AI-related stocks in the S&P 500, which today represent 45% of the index’s market cap. They estimate that these stocks have accounted for 78% of S&P 500 returns, 66% of earnings growth, and 71% of capital spending growth since ChatGPT launched in November 2022. Let that sink in...the narrow concentration over the last three years is astounding. Keeping up with the S&P 500 without leaning heavily into AI has been a virtual impossibility, per the table below. Excluding these 42 AI-related companies, the S&P 500 would have underperformed Europe, Japan, and China over the period.

Mega cap technology companies dominate the MSCI World Index as well, accounting for 9 of the top 10 holdings (all are U.S. companies). The High-Tech Strategist points out that “The U.S. accounts for just 4% of the world’s population and 24% of world GDP, yet these [top] 10 stocks account for 28.3% of the total MSCI World Index weighting. The total weighting of all U.S. stocks comes to 72.7% of the index. NVIDIA (NVDA) alone accounts for 6% of the index weight, more than all Japan’s stocks (5.5%). In country weights, Japan is second to the U.S.” This can be attributed to unwavering enthusiasm for just about anything that touches AI.

Returns, earnings and capex / R&D of AI-related stocks in the S&P 500 since ChatGPT launch in Q4 2022

Direct AI28 stocks AI Utilities8 stocks AI Cap Equip6 stocks Total AI42 stocks S&P 500ex-AI MSCIEurope MSCIJapan MSCIChina
Since November 2022
Price Return 195% 66% 174% 190% 26% 33% 59% 50%
Earnings growth 159% 64% 155% 153% 19% 4% 52% 15%
Capex / R&D growth 72% 13% 20% 68% 19% 28% 37% 27%

Share of changes since November 2022

Price Return 76% 0.8% 1.3% 78% 22%
Earnings growth 63% 1.6% 1.5% 66% 34%
Capex / R&D growth 70% 1.0% 0.2% 71% 29%
Source: Bloomberg, JPMAM, December 22, 2025

As it relates to the impact on the U.S. economy, JPM estimates tech sector capital spending contributed 40%-45% of U.S. GDP growth through the first 9 months of the year, up from less than 5% during the same period in 2023. As JPM depicts to the right, tech capex in 2025 dwarfs every major historical U.S. infrastructure project as a percentage of GDP. Well-known economist David Rosenberg pegs the AI impact on the economy as even greater, writing that “100% of the [1H25] GDP growth has come from the 8% chunk of the economy linked to the AI boom ... the other 92% is completely flat.” This is consistent with the weakness we are seeing elsewhere in the economy (i.e., employment, housing, manufacturing, construction, transportation). There are clearly cracks beneath the surface, with underlying economic growth much less robust than it appears.

As we wrote in our last letter, we see tremendous long-term potential for AI, but question whether the enormous amount of capital being spent will generate attractive returns and over what time frame. The numbers thrown around continue to mystify. OpenAI, for example, is looking to spend $1.4 trillion on AI infrastructure by 2033. Peak annual project percent of GDP

Ben is an exceptional leader and has done an outstanding job during his 7+ years as a Research Analyst and a member of our Portfolio Management Committee (PMC). Thank you for your continued support of Fiduciary Management, Inc.

Original Post

data.title: Mawer Investment Management Q4 2025 Quarterly Update
data.description:
Market Overview

Global equities advanced further in the fourth quarter, and leadership broadened as investors rotated away from U.S. mega-cap AI winners toward cyclicals. Canadian equities outperformed U.S. equities, benefiting from strong commodity gains led principally by gold, while European equities also substantially outpaced U.S. equities, supported by semiconductor stocks and banks, which benefitted from a steeper yield curve, as well as mining companies, which were lifted by this year’s commodity price boom.

AI remained the dominant market narrative, yet the year’s shift from a focus on computing power to concerns about data centre profitability and power supply raised bubble concerns. History offers uncomfortable parallels with past technological booms, from railroads and autos to the late‑1990s dot‑com bust, which developed over several years and were often amplified by retail participation, leverage, and easy credit.

Central banks continued to underpin risk assets, with most major institutions either cutting rates earlier in the year or now on hold as inflation steadied, keeping financial conditions broadly supportive. At the same time, the U.S. dollar weakened notably over the year against most major currencies as interest-rate differentials narrowed and investors grew more concerned about the U.S. political outlook, adding another tailwind for non‑U.S. assets.

Bond markets continued to adjust to this new regime. Intermediate and longer‑term government yields in most developed markets drifted higher as investors reassessed the trade‑off between persistent inflation pressures and ongoing monetary accommodation, while shorter‑dated U.S. yields moved lower following the latest Fed cut. Canadian yields were relatively stable, leaving North American curves steeper. Against this backdrop of easier global policy and unresolved geopolitical and trade risks, gold remained well supported.

How Did We Do?

Our quality-oriented portfolios were, for the most part, unable to keep pace with their benchmarks during another strong quarter for equities. While an environment of rapidly rising, higher-risk stocks tends not to favour an approach focused on sustainable cash-flow generation, diversification, and downside risk management, this same discipline helped protect capital during the heightened volatility earlier in 2025.

Given the strong relative and absolute performance of Canadian equities over the quarter and through most of 2025, our long-standing underweight to gold stocks, just as gold-related names became a key driver of the Canadian market, led to underperformance versus the benchmark this quarter. Over the year, we narrowed this underweight in a measured way as geopolitical uncertainty, de-dollarization, fiscal indiscipline, central bank gold purchases, and falling interest rates created a more supportive backdrop for gold. We have focused on gold-related companies with differentiated, relatively lower-risk business models than traditional producers, and the portfolio now holds four such stocks, which in our assessment provide useful diversification and the potential to remain highly profitable across a wide range of gold prices.

On the positive side for Canadian equities this quarter, four of our holdings in the financials sector (TD (TD:CA), RBC (RY:CA), Scotiabank (BNS:CA), and Manulife (MFC:CA)) posted strong stock performance on the back of stronger-than-expected earnings due to solid net interest income, resilient credit quality, healthy capital markets, and wealth-management fees. Shares of global heavy equipment distributor Finning (FTT:CA) rose after reporting quarterly results that were well above expectations, while shares of autonomous underwater vehicle technology company Kraken Robotics (PNG:CA) also benefited from increased government defence spending. Detracting from performance were holdings of Constellation Software (CSU:CA) and spinoff Topicus (TOI:CA) despite strong underlying earnings amid concerns that AI could affect their software offerings.

In the U.S., Alphabet (GOOGL), the parent company of Google (GOOGL), continued its strong performance as concerns about search engine competition and the resolution of its antitrust case faded, reinforcing its position as a key contributor to the portfolio. Similarly, Amphenol (APH), a leading manufacturer of electronic connectors and cables used in communications, data centers and industrial equipment, benefited from robust demand for AI-related interconnect products, which now account for over a third of its revenue making it another standout performer. Both companies, which were our largest positions at the start of the year, were top contributors to the portfolio’s results. Elsewhere, Waters (WAT) reported solid results supported by growth in Asia, and we increased the position following its acquisition of Becton Dickinson (BDX)’s biosciences and diagnostics business. Danaher (DHR), a science and diagnostics company, delivered margin expansion and strong forward guidance following productivity initiatives, while Cencora (COR), a pharmaceutical distributor, reported solid growth driven by improved sales of specialty and generic pharmaceuticals.

In contrast, insurance brokers Marsh & McLennan (MMC) and Arthur J. Gallagher (AJG) continued to face pressure amid a softer insurance market, despite renewal premiums holding in positive territory. Microsoft (MSFT) paused after a strong run as sentiment cooled and investors questioned the pace of AI spending, while Aptar (ATR), a specialist in drug-delivery and consumer-dispensing solutions, experienced reduced demand from pharmaceutical customers due to inventory normalization in nasal decongestants.

Within international equities, many of the full-year top performers experienced pullbacks this quarter, including defence contractors such as the UK’s BAE, Italy’s Leonardo, and France’s Thales, which declined on the possibility of a diplomatic breakthrough in the Russia-Ukraine war. Geopolitical events early in the year have helped defence company shares more-than-offset the declines seen in the fourth quarter. Elsewhere in Asia, shares of Tencent, a leading Chinese internet and online services platform, and Tencent Music, a major digital music streaming and entertainment provider, surrendered part of their strong year-to-date advance amid a broader bout of weakness in Chinese stocks, even though Chinese equities, in Canadian-dollar terms, still ranked among the best-performing major markets in 2025.

Partially offsetting these negatives, high-bandwidth memory leader SK Hynix nearly doubled in the quarter thanks to explosive demand for its products. Other AI-linked companies were rewarded for continued fundamental strength too, such as TSMC, Kokusai Electric, rail kit manufacturer King Slide, and Japanese conglomerate Hitachi, which produces electrical grid equipment.

Within Canadian bonds, negative returns over the quarter were driven primarily by marginally higher government bond yields as investors remained cautious about increased government debt issuance. Like last quarter, those invested in our global credit opportunities strategy saw a positive return, benefiting from slightly lower short-dated government bond yields and slightly tighter credit spreads.

The balanced strategy maintains a neutral asset mix, reflecting rising valuation concerns amid lingering questions about the true health of the economy. Recent portfolio moves included a slight reduction in equity due to concerns on valuation. This was accompanied by a modest shift from Canadian small cap into larger-cap Canadian equities, consistent with a view that there is a broader opportunity set of high-quality businesses at the larger end of the market. We also made an increase in U.S. equities that we sourced from emerging markets, and to a lesser extent, our international equity allocations. The net effect of this being a slightly lower overall equity weight and, at the margin, less direct exposure to AI-related themes as a result of our underlying equity strategy exposures. Fixed income remains appealing as global central banks pivot toward easing, yields stay elevated versus history, and softer labour data increases the likelihood that interest rates will trend lower, all of which could be supportive for bond returns.

Looking Ahead

As 2025 closes, investors find themselves confronting a familiar blend of optimism and doubt. The powerful combination of AI innovation, continued fiscal largesse, and gradually easing monetary policy has sustained both growth and market confidence longer than many thought possible. Yet, beneath the surface, the contours of this expansion are shifting. It’s increasingly clear that the world’s economies are being rewired—by policy, by technology, and by the rise of populist politics.

Artificial intelligence remains central to productivity hopes and portfolio positioning, but the way that promise is being financed and valued is becoming more demanding. Debt funding is playing a larger role in the data center buildout, and while balance sheets remain solid overall, investors are paying closer attention to the growing amount of debt issued by some technology companies. The combination of industrial‑scale spending, still‑unproven economics, and higher valuations increases the risk that expectations get ahead of reality.

On the policy front, what began as emergency stimulus now defines the modern economic landscape. Fiscal measures once reserved for crises including direct transfers, industrial subsidies, and central bank balance sheet expansion, have all become standing features. Legislators continue to pair expansive spending with tax incentives, reinforcing the impression that growth can be engineered. The result is another wave of fiscal support heading into 2026, even as debt sustainability increasingly comes into question.

Monetary policy, rather than acting as a counterweight, continues to accommodate this fiscal momentum. Liquidity remains abundant, yet long‑term yields stay elevated as investors price persistent deficits and the slow erosion of money’s real value. If productivity gains from AI surprise to the upside, the challenge may not be stagnation but overheating.

For investors, the combination of political ambition and easy liquidity has kept markets buoyant, yet dependent on continued policy support. Credit spreads are tight and risk appetite strong, but loose conditions still reward speculation before discipline, as we’ve arguably seen over the last two years of the cycle. As the cycle matures, assets with genuine scarcity, resilient margins, and prudent capital allocation are likely to lead.

Our focus remains on quality, balance, and durability. Cycles will turn and policies will shift, but companies with steady cash flow and disciplined capital management tend to endure. In a world leaning on policy and promise, patience and perspective may prove the rarest and most valuable advantage.

data.title: Rolls-Royce: Why Lower Valuation Multiples Still Support My Buy Rating
data.description:
Rolls-Royce stock (RYCEF, RYCEY) has advanced 13% since my last report. While the stock did not reach my $18.69, it's a good moment to assess whether the aerospace and defense stock remains attractive, as the price target is governed by the aerospace and industry valuation multiple, which has contracted since my last report. We note that a potential downgrade of the stock would not in the slightest be a negative thing, as the stock has surged 483% since I initiated coverage with a buy rating in November 2023.

Potential Valuation Risks for Rolls-Royce

The main reason to review the rating and price targets is because the price target of Rolls-Royce is driven by a valuation in line with the aerospace and defense industry peers. That multiple has declined from 24.6x in October to around 23.5x today. I believe there's no strong reason for Rolls-Royce to trade at a premium to peers, and that means that the stock price has become more susceptible to aerospace and defense valuation volatility. To maintain a buy rating, it's important that the stock still show substantial upside toward FY27 despite the lower trading multiple. One risk to the valuation besides the lower trading multiple is the Airbus A350 program. Ramp-up of production has been slower than anticipated, and while I do believe that lower OEM sales are not necessarily bad given that production of these engines is not a big profit maker, it also means that the installed base grows at a slower rate, and that does reduce the more profitable aftermarket sales bundled in the TotalCare package, which is a power-by-the-hour structured agreement with operators.

In November, the company provided a trading update reaffirming the full-year guidance for profits between £3.1bn and £3.2bn and free cash flow between £3.0bn and £3.1bn, supported by 8% growth in engine flight hours in the first 10 months of the year.

Modest Margin Expansion With Solid Free Cash Flow Conversion

For 2025, EBITDA margins are expected to increase substantially from 18.6% to 21%, with modest margin expansion in the years after to 21.6%. Capital expenditures are expected to increase modestly, but operating cash flow is expected to exceed the increase in capital expenditures, driving up free cash flow with stable cash flow conversion around 75%. Capital expenditures as a percentage of operating cash flow are expected to be around 21% in the coming years. Shareholder returns are expected to be around 50% in 2025 and 2026, with a significant increase in 2027 to over 75%. I believe that's a healthy return to shareholders as the company remains in a net cash position with strong liquidity, and Rolls-Royce could even decide to operate the business using lower cash balances, which could unlock additional share repurchases.

Rolls-Royce Buy Rating Reiterated

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Despite the lower trading multiple for the aerospace and defense industry, I'm maintaining my buy rating for Rolls-Royce with a $20.26 price target, indicating 16% upside as we shift the focus from 2026 to 2027. We see that the stock has just 3% upside with earnings for 2026 in mind, but I do believe that it makes sense to look at 2027 to assess upside and maintain a forward-looking approach. EBITDA estimates have remained more or less unchanged for 2025 and 2027, but have been revised up by 2.1% for 2027, indicating a total upward revision of 0.6% for EBITDA with a CAGR of 15.1%. Free cash flow estimates have come down by around 2.2% for 2025 but have been revised up by 5.7% for 2026 and by 4.2% for 2027 for a total upward revision of 2.7% and a CAGR of 6.2%. So, while the aerospace and defense industry multiple declined, I remain bullish on the stock as we shift the focus from targets supported by 2026 earnings to targets supported by 2027 levels, and there's a slight uptick in the expected financial performance for Rolls-Royce.

Conclusion: Rolls-Royce Easy Upside Materialized, But There Still Is Some Left

While I considered the possibility that a contraction of the peer trading multiple would result in a change of the rating, this did not happen. The easy upside for Rolls-Royce has materialized, and we do note that there's a risk that volatility in the peer group trading multiples could pressure upside. However, with the current trading multiple of the peer group, which I believe is not unreasonable, we see that upside remains as we pull expected results for 2027 into the price target, and there has been a modest increase in expected EBITDA and free cash flow performance.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Weird Breadth From Small Businesses
data.description:
This morning's release from the NFIB indicated a modest uptick in small business optimism to end 2025. The headline index rose from 99 to 99.5, which is just above the historical median reading.

In the table below, we show each of the nine components that are inputs to the Optimism Index in addition to the eight other indices included with the report. As shown, the increase in the headline number was despite negative breadth among its inputs.

Only two indices rose versus three that declined; however, the gainers were oversized, including a 9-point jump in expectations for the economy to improve. That was a top decile monthly gain for that index, and brings it into the top quintile of readings historically.

As for the indices that are not inputs for the headline number, breadth was much stronger, as only two indices fell versus five that rose. That includes multiple strong readings from indices that track observed changes rather than expectations.

The largest negative impact on overall optimism in December was expectations for real sales to be higher. That index fell 5 points month over month, which is not anything too catastrophic. As shown below, readings remain net positive, and the December print is in the middle of the past several months' range.

Actual sales changes, on the other hand, remains net negative. In other words, more firms continue to report falling instead of rising sales; however, the one point jump in December on top of a four-point increase in November marks some improvement in recent months.

Like actual sales changes, observed earnings changes also remain in net negative territory. Granted, December also saw improvement in this category. For those reporting lower earnings, sales volumes receive the bulk of the finger-pointing.

That reading ticked up to 14%, which was the highest share of responses since June. Meanwhile, higher costs revisited the low end of the past few years' range. In other words, it appears weaker demand was the cause for lower earnings.

Firms reported an increase in capital outlays in the December report, as that reading ended the year at one of the higher levels of the second half. The report offers insights into what companies are putting those expenditures towards, and as shown below, there was an increase in spend across categories save for equipment.

Vehicles saw the biggest jump as that category registered the highest reading since the first quarter. Buildings and land typically get a seasonal bump in December, and last month's reading of 19% was the highest since December 2023, which tied the record high in data going back over a decade.

Turning over to the most important problems, the single largest concern is a combination of labor quality and costs. Only one point behind it is government related concerns (taxes combined with requirements and regulations).

As shown below, those government concerns have risen steadily over the past few years as inflation has gone by the wayside, and December saw a combined 3 percentage point jump, which is the largest one-month increase since the start of the tariff turmoil of the spring.

As for labor concerns, there was a huge surge in those reporting quality of labor as their biggest issue in October (the reading went from 18% to 27%), but since then, that reading has almost completely reversed, as it came in at 19% in December. Cost of labor pulled back one percentage point to 9% in December, which is basically inline with the past year's readings.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Docusign: It's Not The E-Signatures, It's The Integrations (And I'm Buying)
data.description:
Docusign (DOCU) is one of the more beaten-down names in the software sector. The vast majority of software stocks appear to be bruised by the emergence of various vibe coding and productivity products by Anthropic and others. The drastic shift in sentiment has created opportunity for those courageous enough to sift through the rubble. DOCU is one of the cheaper names in my coverage and looks highly buyable here. I like the net cash balance sheet, GAAP profitability, and committed share repurchase program. I suspect the market may be underestimating the resilience of being deeply integrated and entrenched at existing customers. I'm upgrading DOCU to a strong buy rating.

DOCU Stock Price

I last covered DOCU in July, where I reiterated my buy rating due to the then-temporary weakness. That weakness has proven to not be so temporary, as the stock has underperformed the market by more than 27% since.

After a brief stint in the limelight, DOCU has returned to being one of the cheaper names in enterprise software.

DOCU Stock Key Metrics

DOCU is one of the more famous e-signature companies, and that may have put it in the crosshairs of generative AI. Or at least that’s what the market appears to believe. I take the other end of that argument. While e-signature verification itself might be something one could feasibly “vibe code” and manage themselves, the true value of the business is instead in its deep integrations. As one of the true leaders in the market, DOCU is deeply integrated across all of the major platforms, and I'm of the view that customers are in no hurry to tear out and rebuild these valuable integrations.

In the most recent quarter, DOCU delivered 8% YoY revenue growth to $818 million, exceeding guidance of $808 million. I suspect investors may have been disappointed, as they may have been hoping for a return to double-digit growth, but this is moving in the wrong direction.

The company did however see billings growth come in at 10% YoY, a solid result but decelerating meaningfully from the 13% rate of the second quarter. Management did note that they will no longer report this metric in the next fiscal year. There's some irony in that, given that I suspect some investors may have been optimistic after the second quarter results due to this very metric.

On the profitability front, the company delivered solid margin expansion, with non-GAAP operating margins rising 180 bps.

The company has also posted solid margin expansion on a GAAP basis. Operating leverage can come very quickly at these software names if and when the management teams decide to let it.

DOCU ended the quarter with $1 billion of cash and investments vs. no debt. After the recent slide, the net cash position is now more significant and makes up around 7% of the market cap. The company repurchased $215.1 million of stock in the quarter, which I view as aggressive as it represents the vast majority of non-GAAP operating income. DOCU has entered this period of weakness from a position of strength in terms of shareholder returns and the focus on profitability.

Looking ahead, management guided the company to see around 7% YoY growth in revenue. The target for non-GAAP operating margin looks understated and may be what management is more looking to beat.

On the conference call, management noted that their AI-powered product, Intelligent Asset Management (‘IAM’), is on pace “to represent a low double-digit percentage of recurring revenue at year-end.” This is significant because management has noted “early strong retention rates in our first IAM renewal cohorts as well as the continued trend of IAM customers increasing their eSignature usage after moving to the IAM platform.” This would run counter to the argument that DOCU is a loser of generative AI. I continue to have the view that customers will be most eager to use generative AI products at their existing software vendors instead of trying out new ones or attempting to build their own. Management noted that the guidance for decelerating growth incorporates the “hard comp” coming up, though I suspect that investors should expect the upcoming fiscal year to see similar if not slower growth rates. Management did note that they view this as a “great opportunity” to lean into their buyback, which I view positively given the low stock valuation.

Is DOCU stock a Buy, Sell, or Hold?

At recent prices, DOCU traded at around 16x forward earnings.

Consensus estimates call for quite modest operating leverage, with topline growth expected to come in the mid-single-digit range.

It's refreshing to see consensus estimates factor in deceleration next year - I would be more concerned if investors were expecting an acceleration. The company has been razor focused on profitability and share repurchases in recent years. As a result, I expect the company to deliver faster operating leverage than peers, perhaps even more urgently given the struggling stock valuation as well as the potential for decelerating topline growth. I expect GAAP net margins to reach around 30% at the very least, if not much higher. Non-GAAP operating margins are already above 30%, and with GAAP operating margins now in the double digits, just a couple of years of operating leverage would bridge the gap. That places the valuation at around 13x long term earnings, which I consider to be quite cheap given the expanding GAAP profit margins and net cash balance sheet. I can see this re-rating to at least 16x long-term earnings, if not into the 20x range, due to the strong balance sheet. That suggests strong multiple expansion upside in addition to the ongoing top-line growth and GAAP earnings yield.

The main question to ask here is whether generative AI will really disrupt the business. My assumption is that there may be some impact, but not nearly to the extent that the market is pricing in. Price competition has been present for the entirety of DOCU’s existence, and a quick Google search can easily confirm that. Yet DOCU has continued to grow with its customers due not to its e-signature solution but instead to the integration platform that comes along with it. I expect the same to occur with generative AI. Perhaps DOCU might need to reduce some costs, especially with regard to the plain e-signature component of its platform, but I suspect that it will still post solid growth due to already being entrenched with its customer base. I also wouldn’t rule out the company being able to offset any potential weakness through new generative AI-powered products such as IAM.

DOCU Stock Risks

It's possible that DOCU really does get disrupted by generative AI. Perhaps some customers move some usage off platform or are able to easily replicate all the integrations with a cheaper product. It's possible that management engages in expensive M&A that destroys shareholder value. It's possible that the company will need to reduce prices in order to retain customers, leading to pressure on my hoped-for margin expansion.

DOCU Stock Conclusion

It's highly possible that DOCU does see growth rates decelerate, perhaps due in part to generative AI. That said, the current valuation is arguably already pricing this in. The GAAP profitability, net cash balance sheet, and mature capital allocation policies are underappreciated in the valuation. I expect growth rates to remain resilient due to the deep integrations of the platform. DOCU is the kind of stock I look for in the Best of Breed Growth Stocks Portfolio. I'm upgrading the stock to strong buy.

data.title: Bitcoin And Altcoins Breakout As Stock Market Momentum Fades: BTC, ETH And SOL Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Crypto is back in beast mode today after a 1.5-month-long consolidation across all major tokens. Digital assets have a funny way of fading into the background when interest and prices drop, often following a few crashes or scandals. And make no mistake-the latest correction was no small matter.

Bitcoin (BTC-USD), the most sought-after and respected crypto, corrected by 36%, with the total crypto market cap suffering a similar drop. Others were hit even harder, with 50% corrections seen in XRP (XRP-USD) and Solana (SOL-USD), and brutal 70% drops in names like Cardano (ADA-USD).

But zooming out, cryptos actually led the sensational rally that eventually spread to other assets. Bitcoin kicked off the 2024-2025 frenzy as early as January 2024, trading at $40,000 before peaking at $126,000 19 months later - a +3X increase.

As a matter of fact, looking at past cycles, cryptocurrencies have often acted as a leading indicator, rising and falling weeks or even months before other asset classes. This happens for a simple technical reason: they sit at the very top of the risk spectrum.

Consequently, they are the first to see demand dry up when uncertainty arises - conditions we saw clearly during the October-November period as layoffs rose and fears of an "AI Peak" bloomed. So, where do things stand now?

Digital assets have been largely off the radar since November, with volatility staying surprisingly contained. However, with stocks hovering at all-time highs and metals posting record performances, cryptocurrencies could be primed for some mean reversion higher.

As geopolitical uncertainty looms and the Fed's independence comes under fire yet again, traders may look to crypto for risk diversification in assets that have corrected already. Expect things to remain volatile in this environment.

Let's dive right into the daily charts and technical levels for Bitcoin, Ethereum (ETH-USD), and Solana (SOL-USD).

Bitcoin Daily Chart and Technical Levels

Bitcoin is now breaching its 50-day moving average on strong momentum after an initial test last week. Supported by the formation of an uptrend (higher lows, strong trendline) and confirmed by a bounce on neutral from the RSI Index, the path for a higher retest is forming.

Two tests will be coming for Bitcoin:

  • Breaking the January 5 highs ($94,810) - short-term test (looks close to breaking)
  • Passing the test of the $100,000 resistance.
    • This one will need a review if prices get there. A huge psychological resistance that will surely dictate risk appetite for cryptos in 2026.

Levels of interest for BTC trading:

Support Levels:

  • $88,000 to $93,000 major support turned pivot (breaking out)
  • 50-Day MA ($92,200, broken this morning)
  • Past Week Lows and Uptrend: $89,340
  • $85,000 mid-term support (+/- $1,500)
  • $75,000 key long-term support

Resistance Levels:

  • Highs to break $94,810 (short-term resistance)
  • $98,000 to $100,000 Resistance
  • $104,000 200-Day MA
  • Resistance at previous ATH: $106,000 to $108,000
  • Current ATH resistance: $124,000 to $126,000

Ethereum Daily Chart and Technical Levels

Ethereum is now looking a bit less strong compared to its older brother, but this could still be quite positive. A triangle formation will help to spot the direction of an imminent breakout.

Showing similar RSI developments as BTC, Ethereum should see its price blaze as long as bulls manage to break above its 200-day moving average, coming in as an immediate test ($3,216). A rise in ETH could also help the broader altcoin market to rally.

data.title: Northrop Grumman: $1.5 Trillion Defense Budget Boosts F/A-XX Win (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Northrop Grumman stock (NOC) has gained almost 20% since my last report, partially driven by increased global tension and the prospects of a $1.5 trillion defense budget floated by President Trump. I believe that a substantially higher defense budget could help Northrop Grumman secure a win for the F/A-XX, which is the naval equivalent of the Next Generation Air Dominance, or NGAD, program. In this report, I discuss why I believe such a budget would drive a downselect for the program and why I believe Northrop Grumman should be that winner.

A Look at the Naval NGAD and Why Northrop Grumman Should Be the Winner

The F-35 currently is the most advanced fighter jet in service with the US and its allies. Development, however, was complex, as there was an effort to have one airframe concept perform tasks for several using services. That led to a complex engineering process and product with a loss of commonality between the F-35. That also drives up operational costs and eliminates cost reductions through the use of a common airframe.

Involved parties seemed to have learned from that, as the development of the next-generation fighter jet, which is a sixth-generation fighter jet, was split into a US Navy program known as the F/A-XX program and a program for the US Air Force. For the US Air Force program, there initially were three contenders, namely Boeing (BA), Lockheed Martin (LMT) and Northrop Grumman. Northrop Grumman withdrew from the competition in 2023. In fact, they never seriously entered that competition. That left only two contenders, and Boeing was selected last year.

That Boeing was selected might not have made sense to everyone given the substantial cost overruns on defense programs and delays on both commercial and defense programs. However, the aerospace and defense industry thrives on a competitive element, not only a competitive edge over adversaries but also within the industrial complex to get the most capable product at the best price. Boeing currently has the F/A-18 and the F-15EX in its fighter jet portfolio. F/A-18 production is set to end by 2027, with only sustainment and upgrade work carried out through 2030, while the F-15EX is an iterative design that's not the most advanced fighter jet available and unlikely to be produced in large quantities. In order to keep design and manufacturing knowledge available, Boeing was the front-runner in the competition with Lockheed Martin, which already has the F-35 in its portfolio, which likely will see production and sustainment efforts for decades to come.

Switching to the US Navy's sixth-generation fighter jet, we note that Northrop Grumman has been pursuing this project. Lockheed Martin was eliminated from the competition, likely because it already has the F-35 work. This leaves Boeing and Northrop Grumman as the only contenders. Applying the same principle of preserving know-how and utilizing engineering resources, I believe Northrop Grumman would be most likely to win. Boeing has the engineering resources required for the F-47, although Boeing believes it can do both projects. Northrop Grumman has engineering resources coming off the B-21 program as the program has been through the most demanding phase for engineering resources. Part of the engineering resources is likely required for the Sentinel, the next-generation land-based intercontinental ballistic missile, but other than that, the company should have resources available, and we note that the B-21, in terms of production units, is a substantially smaller program compared to the fighter jets. For that reason, it would make sense to select Northrop Grumman and ultimately have three companies with design and manufacturing know-how for fighter jets, essentially adding Northrop Grumman back to the mix.

How the Higher Defense Budgets Could Help Northrop Grumman

In what I deemed a surprising move, in June 2025 the Pentagon cut prospective funding for the F/A-XX program from $972 million to $74 million, which would essentially not be enough to make a downselection any time soon. Most of the funds were transferred to the F-47 program with the possibility that the F/A-XX would become a model or variant of the F-47. Given the complications that came with the F-35, I strongly believe that would not be the most prudent decision.

In October 2025, Congress assigned $750 million to speed up the F/A-XX program for earlier fielding, and $1.4 billion was earmarked for the program in 2026, largely undoing the Pentagon’s F/A-XX cut. The $1.4 billion in funding matches the Navy’s “wish list” for unfunded projects. With these funds, a selection can be made. However, to keep the program up to speed in the Engineering & Manufacturing Development, or EMD, phase, billions of dollars are required annually. So, the intervention from Congress was enough to allow for a downselection and some early R&D work, but it would not be enough to provide solid funding for the R&D phase, which I believe would require at least $2 billion per year.

President Trump now has floated the idea of increasing the defense budget from around $900 billion to $1.5 trillion, marking a 66.7% increase. Historically, the branches of the US Armed Forces have been underfunded compared to the capabilities they want as well as the quantities of equipment to be procured. That has led to programs cannibalizing each other, in the same way that was almost set to happen to the F/A-XX program when the Pentagon shifted funds from the F/A-XX program to the F-47 program. So, there's friction between R&D funds and procurement funds, and within both buckets there has been friction between allocations toward certain programs. So, to put it simply, many programs are aiming for the same buck in the budget.

Especially at this moment, there's substantial pressure within R&D as there are various efforts ongoing:

  • NGAD
  • Hypersonic
  • Golden Dome missile defense
  • Development of cyber capabilities
  • High-energy laser weapon systems
  • Drones and counter-drone defense
  • Nuclear modernization

Having a substantially bigger defense budget would substantially increase the chances for the F/A-XX to receive appropriate funding, giving the program a more credible and committed development path. The US defense budgets include a Future Year Defense Program, or FYDP, planning, and with a downselection and a credible development path, the outyear funding projections can be made, paving the way for acquisition planning and assigning funding for program elements. In essence, the FYDP is what makes many hardware programs possible, and it often also makes it more difficult to eliminate programs in the future. There could even be an advance procurement authority for future production. With strong backing for the budget, contracts can also be awarded that lean on future funding. While the F/A-XX program is not the only program to benefit from a higher defense budget, it does substantially remove competition for the same buck, and adds a credible path from downselection to EMD to procurement.

The Risk of a $1.5 Trillion Defense Budget

A $1.5 trillion defense budget would be extremely good news for the F/A-XX program, given that both next-generation fighter jet programs have seen severe funding challenges in the past. At the same time, we should point out that a $1.5 trillion budget is not certain, and it would more than likely add substantial debt for the US.

Northrop Grumman Stock Barely Scores A Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Northrop Grumman is not the most impressive stock in terms of expected growth. The company is expected to have a CAGR of 4.3% on sales, 6.1% on EBITDA, and 10.7% on free cash flow. These growth rates are measured from 2024. If we take 2025 as the base year, the sales growth and EBITDA growth are expected to be better at 5.5% and 7.7%, but free cash flow growth will only be 5.9%, driven by higher capital expenditures. Total EBITDA estimates improved by 2.7%, while free cash flow estimates have been revised up by 1.1%. Northrop Grumman has a long-term EV/EBITDA of around 12.5x, which would imply substantial downside. However, we note that the company has been averaging closer to 15.5x EV/EBITDA in recent years. Using that as the EV/EBITDA multiple indicates that the stock is currently fairly valued, which I believe does make sense given that the market should be forward looking. If we look ahead now, we should look at the fundamentally supported stock prices for 2027, which would imply a $682.43 price target with 10% upside, which is the bare minimum required for a buy rating. We do note that the stock trades at a substantial discount to peers, and potentially there's additional upside if the multiple expands.

If we look at the EBITDA margin progression, we expect modest improvement in the margins and cash conversion rates around 52%-54% and capital expenditures at 31% of operating cash flow. I expect around $1.5 billion in annual share repurchases, bringing the return on free cash flow to 84%-88%. The company is expected to maintain a cash balance of around $4-$4.3 billion, with net-debt leverage dropping from 2.44x in 2024 to 1.8x in 2027, potentially allowing for $1.3 billion in additional share repurchases by 2027, targeting a 2x net debt leverage.

Conclusion: Brighter F/A-XX Prospects, Stock Barely Scores a Buy Rating

I believe that a higher defense budget would substantially increase the prospects of the F/A-XX program gaining budget and planning credibility. I also believe that in order to maintain a diverse and competitive industry, Northrop Grumman is the best choice to develop the next generation fighter jet for the US Navy. At the same time, we note that a substantial increase in the defense budget will result in substantial increases in debt for the US government, so a substantial increase in the defense budget is no certainty. From a valuation perspective, we note that Northrop Grumman is not a high growth company, nor is the stock extremely cheap, but the stock continues to trade at a discount to peers while substantial execution risk on the B-21 is now behind us. As a result, I'm upgrading the stock to buy.
